targetSlots.planningTest: Show what the protagonist wants, lacks, or cannot tolerate before the story changes.
Present the concrete path into the story problem.
End the opening with a choice or consequence that prevents easy retreat.
Reveal information or opposition that changes what the protagonist thinks the story is.
Confront the protagonist with the flaw, false belief, or missing knowledge.
Make victory depend on a meaningful choice, not only force or luck.

concept.premise: Sariq Nahl, a clockwork caravan guide, inherits a broken pilgrimage automaton that predicts where wells will vanish. To save his drought-struck city, he must cross a treaty desert with rivals who believe the machine is stealing water from the future.
concept.readerPromise: A desert pilgrimage fantasy adventure about scarcity, prophecy, and whether survival justifies borrowing from tomorrow.
concept.centralConflict: Sariq needs the automaton's predictions to find water, while the treaty clans hunt anyone using machinery that changes future wells.

protagonist.name: Sariq Nahl
protagonist.desire: earn a caravan license and bring water rights back to his city
protagonist.fear: discovering his guidance saves clients by condemning unseen travelers
protagonist.flaw: he relies on calculations to avoid admitting whose suffering he is choosing

characters.name: Amina Resh
characters.materiality: A treaty-clan scout who can lead Sariq through taboo routes if he shares the machine's truth.

characters.name: Dalen Voss
characters.materiality: A merchant sponsor whose water contract helps the city but pushes Sariq toward selfish choices.

characters.name: Matriarch Sahel
characters.materiality: A clan leader enforcing the treaty who can destroy the automaton and strand the caravan.

worldFacts.fact: Desert wells vanish from the present when a clockwork prediction draws too much certainty from their future.
The treaty clans mark safe routes with wind bells that sound only for travelers who have shared water honestly.
The pilgrimage automaton calculates routes by consuming a brass memory of every oath spoken near it.

storyPromise.text: If Sariq learns what the automaton costs, he can save the city but must choose whose future water disappears.

constraints: Produce planning contracts only, not prose.
Use scene contracts as the generation and adherence unit.
Make character and world obligations materially affect choices, costs, or consequences.
Keep the diagnostic to six chapter-contract units.
